speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3 of an interview with Regina Clipper, May 17th, 1995, in New York. Juliette Halpern, interviewer. You were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: This lady was sitting there, and she-- I-- she didn't even eat, whatever they gave us. And I spoke to her, and I looked at her. And I said to her, my god, you have lice under your skin. Because there was a lot of lice there in, in-- every morning and every evening, we took the lice out of our clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and she just sat there. And it was terrible to look at her. I ask her, where did you live? She says, in Prague. My husband was a professor at the university.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a terrible camp. People died like flies. It was dirty and filthy and, and corpses all over, all over. It was terrible, terrible. And one friend that I knew in Hindenburg said that she will give me a job cutting hair. She says-- she says, you get two bowls of soup. So fine. I went. They gave me a scissor. And in came transports with young girls. And nobody was watching us, the ones that cut hair. And I just clipped the scissors, and I didn't cut the hair. Boy, did I get it. There was a kapo there, real kooky. Boy, did I get it. And I didn't go to work there anymore. And well, it was-- the time went by. And we were hungry and, and praying the end should come. And it finally came. The beginning-- the 10th of April, it got very quiet in the camp. And nobody-- I mean, you didn't see so many SS and so many SS women. It got quiet. And they took us out of this camp-- they put us in another-- a barrack-- they put us in another barrack. And a lot of people came from which they had sent during the, the-- in March. And among them was Anne Frank, only I didn't know about her then. And, and it-- And everybody, you know, you hear rumors. They, they mined the camp. The Germans are going. They're going to blow up the camp. And we were sitting there. And you can't go to the-- to the wires to go out. It's going to electrocute you. So we were just sitting there. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and everybody was kind of-- they didn't know what, what was happening. We got scared that we were going to be blown up. And the, the end is here, almost there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then all of a sudden, the gates opened, and the Germans that had fled the camp came back in. And behind them came the British. And it-- it's a joke. We were looking out the window. And one girl said, look, the soldiers are back. What do you mean the soldiers are back? Yeah, the Nazis just walked in and tanks are coming in with men in uniform. And we looked. I said, don't you-- what are you blind? These are stars, not swastikas. And they drove in. The liberation, they drove in. And everybody ran, ran, crawled, cried, laughed, fell, and whatever. It's unbelievable that we finally, after five years, we were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the-- you know, came-- they fed us to, to-- they didn't know what to do. They didn't have the experience. And a lot of people died afterwards. And it's, it's a shame. But this is what happened. And a lot of people were very ill. And they were sent to Switzerland and to Sweden. A friend of mine came from Vienna. She had-- she developed TB, I think. And she died in Sweden. But we will liberated. And then they put us in the barracks where the Nazis had lived, you know, soldier barracks with bathrooms and bunk beds and kitchens. And we got food. And we got some clothes. And, and still, we were very unsure what's going to happen to us. And I said to my friend Hanka-- the same girl that gave me the soup in Pionki-- let's get out of here. I had a cousin in Dusseldorf that had a Christian wife, who was Jewish, converted to Judaism. Maybe they went into hiding. Let's go to Dusseldorf. You're crazy, she said. No, let's go. I said, if you don't want to come with me, I'll go by myself. She came with me. And we hitchhiked. And we, we, we went on the train, just hung ourselves on the train there somewhere. And we went first to Celle, and then Hanover. And then we finally got to, to Duisburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Duisburg, we will take-- took the train. And, and while standing-- there were no seating places-- there was a blonde woman. And she looked at us, asked, and she said, are you from the camp? I said, yes. So where you going? I said, we're going to Dusseldorf. You have anybody there? I said, I'm looking for somebody. I said, maybe you'll know if there are some Jews left in the Sudhoff. She says, I really don't know. I only one name, she says, because my friend who works for the DP Chem people. I said, what's his name? She says, Adolf Fischmann. I said, that's the one. Isn't that something?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she sent us to her friend. And the friend gave us directions. And we literally walked to Benrath, which is about 30 miles from the train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to ask you, how did you move around, taking trains? Where did you get money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No money. We had stripes on. We went on the trains. We went on the trains. And they, they let us, too. And one city had a curfew, so we had to sleep somewhere. We went to the police station. We said, we're, we're looking for our family. We're out of concentration camp. We have to sleep somewhere. So they let us sleep. And they gave us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came to the door, and I rang the doorbell, and my tante opened the door. And she looked at me, and she says, oh, my god, Regina. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Anyway, we, we walked in. And my cousin Adolf wasn't home. And he came about 10 minutes later, and a lot of crying, a lot of laughing. And, and he said, well, you have a home now. You're staying here. And to friend he said, Hanka, you have relatives? She says, I didn't find anybody yet. Well, he says, until you find, you stay here, too. You have a home here, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then he went to buy us clothes. And, and the first meal we ate was white bread with apple sauce on it and real coffee. And I still taste it. [LAUGHS] And then we were in Dusseldorf. And you know, we, we, we were free. We, we went to a movie. We met some friends. We went to a synagogue. The synagogue was two rooms in a school building. And we met some people that also came from camp and settled there. And we had-- we, we made a lot of friends. We're still friendly today. And my cousin's daughter was 13 when I came there. She had a tutor. And as she tutored her, I listened in. I read a lot. And I went to Berlitz school to get the fundamental of English. I knew I had an uncle in Palestine, this Uncle Chaim. And I wrote to him. And he, he wrote back to me. And he was in Haganah. And he ca-- I first saw him in 1946. He was the, the head of the Haganah that arranged the illegal immigrants to Israel. And he wanted-- asked me if I wanted to go illegally. Oh, it was in '47, I think. And I said, no. I don't want to end in Cyprus. I had enough of camp. Maybe later. And then 1948, the [INAUDIBLE] of my cousin Willie, Willie Shlomkass-- he lives next door-- she sent me the papers. And the process was very long, long, long. I must have been examined about five times by every doctor and interrogated by whoever. And in 1949, in July-- 19th of July-- I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up to this point, had you met anybody that was able to inform you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what had happened to your own family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Nobody knew. They were all sent away, they told me. And nobody knew. One uncle was-- in an-- in a different-- my father's younger brother was in a different camp and then also an ammunition factory. And he found me in Dusseldorf. But he went illegally to Palestine. And he ended up in Cyprus a long time. After the war in 1968, when I went to-- to-- we went to Israel on a trip. We met everybody. We saw everybody. We were there for three weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I arrived here, my cousin took me in. And I had a home. I was very lucky. I had a home in Germany and a home here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- the whole summer I was on vacation, she says-- said. And then she suggested that I learn hairdressing. There was a course in cosmetology that taught you everything. I went to the Vogue Institute in New York City. And I already knew a little English because I, I learned it-- I mean, I learn languages. And I finished-- I was there from September till May '46. I got my license, and I got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but one thing happened to me that was very strange. The school sent me to New Hyde Park in a very nice salon. And I got the job. And I, I did fine. And I worked a week. And then I got a phone call from this boss on Monday morning that he doesn't mind me working there, but I should always wear a long sleeve uniform. I said, why? Because my customers don't like Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it's not your customers. It's you are a big Nazi. It's a shame, I said, to him. I suffered so much, and I came to a free country. And I encounter the same anti-Semitism. And I hung up and I called the school to tell them about this. I don't know what happened, but they said they wouldn't send anybody to work there. Yeah, I worked. And I saved up a penny. [LAUGHS] And, and then my I met my husband. My cousin Willie found him. [LAUGHS] And we got married in 1952, 17th of February. And we both worked. And we rented a small apartment in Long Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your husband a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My husband is a survivor. He was in Bergen-Belsen also, but I didn't know him then. We met here. And, and in, in 1954, my daughter was born. She was born prematurely, seven months ba-- baby. She was three pounds and five ounces. And she was a month in the incubator. And I got her home. She was four and a half pounds. And she's a beautiful girl and a nice girl. And then in '57 my son Andy was born. And he was the opposite. He was eight pounds and nine ounces. And we worked hard, very hard. And we tried are very best. And I'm very happy I came to the United States [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because it is a free country. And Americans don't realize what freedom means, really, because they've always been free. It is a terrible thing if you cannot say you are Jewish, or you are afraid somebody would hit you over the head. It's-- I don't know why Jews are the scapegoats of the world because they're really good people. And but I'm happy here. I like it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't have too much family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have-- I have a cousin in Brooklyn who survived the war in Russia. And she came here quite a while ago with her husband and two boys. Her husband died. He had a heart attack. She's not too well. She lives in Brooklyn. And I have cousins in New Jersey from my father's side. My cousin Yoram is the head of a trauma unit in New Jersey. And he's, he's a very famous doctor. And his son is there. And I have an aunt still in Israel. I have cousins in Israel. And we try and go there. But we were only there twice, in '68 and in '89. It's expensive, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Socially, what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Socially, I ha-- I have friends. I have a lot of friends, also survivors. My friend Celia, who hid us under the bed, lives in New Hyde Park. My friend Hanka lives now-- lived in Toronto. And she now lives in Miami Beach. My husband has friends also in Florida. We see them. We have a few friends here also, mostly Europeans. But I have some American friends, too. It's-- and we, we go to the Temple. We belong to Temple Emanuel. And we don't go too often-- for the holidays and sometimes Friday night. And we see our son in New Jersey. And he comes here to visit. In the winter time, we rent a place in Florida so we're near to our daughter and our grandchildren. My-- our grandchildren are 10 and eight. Robin is eight, and Aaron is 10. And, of course, like every grandmother who say they are the best and the smartest in the world. [LAUGHS] And, and now my cousin lives next door. She is older. And I'm glad that she is there. And she's glad that I'm here, so. She has nobody. Now everybody-- all her family died. So she has two nephews, though. And the one that looks like Steven Spielberg is her nephew, in the photograph. And we hope to be well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you have busily constructed a new life in the United States, raised a family, what does time put in terms of a perspective on that earlier experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I, I didn't think so much or worry so much. I put it in the back of my mind while we were raising a family. And we had to work very hard. Our children went to college. Both have a master's degree-- my daughter from Houston. And she went to Albany, University of Albany and to University of Houston. She got her master's degree. My son went to state school in Oswego for a ba-- for his bachelors. And he has a master's from Columbia University. And they're doing well. And I'm glad they're doing well. And they have nice-- my daughter has a wonderful husband. And my son has a beautiful, lovely wife. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't think too much about the past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I think about the past, now. Then I didn't think-- I don't think I had time to think. But now, as we get older, we think about it a lot. And we-- and I think all the survivors have that-- who have lost a lot of family-- a very big guilt that we survived and they did not. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] If you ask other people, they'll tell you the same thing, I think. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you are together with friends who are survivors do you talk about these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we talk all, all the time. Remember this? I mean, sometimes we joke about it. We tell the story when we ran Hindenburg, we had to put on a show. And we were in the show. And we made jokes and fun. We sang in, in front of the Nazis. Yeah, now we joke about it. They wanted--

speaker: interviewer
text: You think such a thing could ever happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you something. I am afraid that something sh-- would happen again because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every, every century-- not century-- every decade, some other nuts come up-- the skinheads, the neo-Nazis. Now in Russia, it's no communism. Now they're going to have worse for the Jews. And the Jews should get out of Russia. They have a Nazi monger there that is terrible. And the Russians-- the Eastern Europeans were known for anti-Semitism. The Poles are strict Catholics. And the Catholic church als-- feed-- feeded-- fed-- feeded-- fed this anti-Semitism. And you know, no matter how much you try and it doesn't matter when you're a Jew. If you forget that you're a Jew, somebody will come along and remind you of it. It's true. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Radom was a very rich man. He had a, a leather factory. And he had a foreman that was his-- a Polish man, a Christian. And this Polish man had a son. And this Jewish boss sent this son to the university to become an engineer or a doctor. I don't remember right what. The war broke out and the man asked this Polish man to hide him and his family. He hid them. About a few months later, he went to the Nazis and told them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, it-- you lose-- you lose faith in people. You lose faith. I could never have visualized that Germany-- that the German people would do a thing like this, never. But they did. Maybe the times-- well, after the First World War, they were-- they had a depression. But everybody else had a depression, not just them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they weren't treated right after the First World War. And Hitler came up. And he felt that if he's going to make it judenrein, he's going to become the king of the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which person-- which-- what kind of a mind figures out to annihilate an entire race? And, and they applauded him. They helped him. They, they worked for him. They did everything. There were a few good Germans too. I can't say-- not everybody is bad and not everybody is good. There is always a little-- but to do things like this is horrendous, horrendous what they did-- gas people, children. Take people-- which I saw myself-- take people, babies by the feet and knock their head against the brick wall. Who does this? Animals don't do this. But they did. They did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was standing in line in Poland for, for a loaf of bread with everybody else. And a, a Polish woman said to the, the Nazi, the SS man that was standing there, Jude. She pointed me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came to me and he asked me, are you Jude? And I said, no, I'm a gypsy. My father is a Kapellmeister in the-- in the hotel with a band. But I spoke German. And I don't know why I did this. I had seen the advertisement for the gypsy band. Oh, yeah, he said, he's the conductor? Went in the bakery and gave me two breads. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it could have been different. He could have not liked the gypsy, you know? It was just-- that was just lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to have two things in life-- ma-- mazel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and be at the right place at the right time, and know it's the right time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is also luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also luck. Yeah. And to this day, I do not understand how any of us survived. I can't understand it. People don't know what we went through. We can't even express it all-- what hunger feels. Hunger is the worst thing, the worst. Or your cold. And it is so cold. And you have no clothes. And you shiver. And you cannot sleep. You sleep on wooden-- with seven people. One sleeps on top of the other. Maybe that kept us a little warmer. I don't know. You can't visualize. And you can't understand that people take three young children away from a mother and burn them. And don't let her go along with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a minute.
